poem:
**The Ban**

They nailed a notice to the bright door  
and called it safety.  
Behind it the children kept talking,  
like wind in the wires,  
seven in ten still singing.

The commissioners, the experts—  
stones in the mouth of the state.

No joy in prophecy:  
only the old harm  
finding new paths  
through our good intentions.
prompt: You are the poet RS Thomas. You woke up this morning feeling creative. You have just read this story in the newspaper: "Australia’s teen social media ban is a flop. But there’s no joy in ‘I told you so’ | Samantha Floreani This week, it was revealed that despite the Australian government’s world-first teen social media ban, around seven in 10 children remain on major platforms. What’s more, the eSafety report also shows that there has been no notable change in cyberbullying or image-based abuse reported by children. For a policy that was touted as the solution to keeping kids safe from harm online, this is a damning indictment of the ban’s effectiveness. Who could possibly have predicted that this wasn’t going to work? Well, lots of people. Countless experts were ignored, including those in the fields of digital wellbeing, digital rights advocacy, youth mental health and more than 140 academics and 20 Australian civil society organisations. Even the eSafety commissioner herself had doubts, and internally the government was aware of a lack of evidence to support the ban, before they passed the legislation anyway. I’ve written in the past about some of the ban’s troubles, including the problem of ignoring many young people’s experiences of being online, and the poor policymaking process behind the ban. But there is very little joy in saying “I told you so” when the outcome leaves children - and indeed everyone - worse off. The fallback argument for the social media ban is that it’s better than nothing. But with results like these, it may be worse than nothing, given it potentially creates new problems. Children". Write a poem in YOUR OWN DISTINCTIVE STYLE, no more than 60 words long. You may add a one line title at the top if you like.
news: Australia’s teen social media ban is a flop. But there’s no joy in ‘I told you so’ | Samantha Floreani This week, it was revealed that despite the Australian government’s world-first teen social media ban, around seven in 10 children remain on major platforms. What’s more, the eSafety report also shows that there has been no notable change in cyberbullying or image-based abuse reported by children. For a policy that was touted as the solution to keeping kids safe from harm online, this is a damning indictment of the ban’s effectiveness. Who could possibly have predicted that this wasn’t going to work? Well, lots of people. Countless experts were ignored, including those in the fields of digital wellbeing, digital rights advocacy, youth mental health and more than 140 academics and 20 Australian civil society organisations. Even the eSafety commissioner herself had doubts, and internally the government was aware of a lack of evidence to support the ban, before they passed the legislation anyway. I’ve written in the past about some of the ban’s troubles, including the problem of ignoring many young people’s experiences of being online, and the poor policymaking process behind the ban. But there is very little joy in saying “I told you so” when the outcome leaves children - and indeed everyone - worse off. The fallback argument for the social media ban is that it’s better than nothing. But with results like these, it may be worse than nothing, given it potentially creates new problems. Children
